coredata.dc:title: Nanocomposite of chitosan/gelatin/carbon quantum dots as a biocompatible and efficient nanocarrier for improving the Curcumin delivery restrictions to treat brain cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Ostovar, Shima
Pourmadadi, Mehrab
Shamsabadipour, Amin
Mashayekh, Parsa
coredata.dc:description: Curcumin (CUR) is among the most appropriate and natural-based anticancer drugs that can be applied effectively treat different classes of cancers. However, CUR suffers from a low half-life and stability in the body, which has restricted the efficacy of its delivery applications. This study is dedicated to introducing the pH-sensitive nanocomposite of chitosan (CS)/gelatin (GE)/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an applicable nanocarrier for enhancing CUR half-life and its delivery restrictions. The CS/GE hydrogel was synthesized by the physical crosslinking method, which improves the biocompatibility of this hydrogel. Moreover, the water-in-oil-in-water (W/O/W) double emulsion approach is involved in fabricating the drug-loaded CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanocomposite. Afterward, drug encapsulation (EE) and loading efficiencies (LE) have been determined. Furthermore, FTIR and XRD assessments were performed to confirm the CUR incorporation into the prepared nanocarrier and crystalline features of the nanoparticles. Then, by employing Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis, the size distribution and stability of the drug-loaded nanocomposites have been assessed, which indicated monodisperse and stable nanoparticles. Furthermore, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) was utilized that confirmed the homogeneous distribution of the nanoparticles with smooth and quite spherical structures. In vitro drug release pattern was studied and the kinetic analysis was performed using a curve fitting technique to determine the governing release mechanism at both acidic pH and physiological conditions. The obtained outcomes from release data revealed a controlled release behavior with a 22-hour half-life, while the EE% and EL% were acquired at 46.75 % and 87.5 %, respectively. In addition, the MTT assay has been carried out on U-87 MG cell lines to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the nanocomposite. The findings showed that the fabricated nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs can be assumed as a biocompatible CUR nanocarrier, while the drug-loaded nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs@CUR showed enhanced cytotoxicity compared to the pure CUR. Based on the obtained results, this study suggests the CS/GE/CQDs nanocomposite as a biocompatible and potential nanocarrier for ameliorating CUR delivery restrictions to treat brain cancers.

can be defined the same as the Higuchi kinetic model. Moreover, the exponent (n) is a characteristic exponent to evaluate the governing drug release mechanism. If n is equal to 0.43, it indicates the Fickian diffusion, and when n is between 0.43 and 0.85, the anomalous transport is the major mechanism. Moreover, Case II and super Case II transport mechanisms can be assigned to the upper 0.85 value for n.

head.ce:title: Nanocomposite of chitosan/gelatin/carbon quantum dots as a biocompatible and efficient nanocarrier for improving the Curcumin delivery restrictions to treat brain cancer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Curcumin (CUR) is among the most appropriate and natural-based anticancer drugs that can be applied effectively treat different classes of cancers. However, CUR suffers from a low half-life and stability in the body, which has restricted the efficacy of its delivery applications. This study is dedicated to introducing the pH-sensitive nanocomposite of chitosan (CS)/gelatin (GE)/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an applicable nanocarrier for enhancing CUR half-life and its delivery restrictions. The CS/GE hydrogel was synthesized by the physical crosslinking method, which improves the biocompatibility of this hydrogel. Moreover, the water-in-oil-in-water (W/O/W) double emulsion approach is involved in fabricating the drug-loaded CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanocomposite. Afterward, drug encapsulation (EE) and loading efficiencies (LE) have been determined. Furthermore, FTIR and XRD assessments were performed to confirm the CUR incorporation into the prepared nanocarrier and crystalline features of the nanoparticles. Then, by employing Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis, the size distribution and stability of the drug-loaded nanocomposites have been assessed, which indicated monodisperse and stable nanoparticles. Furthermore, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) was utilized that confirmed the homogeneous distribution of the nanoparticles with smooth and quite spherical structures. In vitro drug release pattern was studied and the kinetic analysis was performed using a curve fitting technique to determine the governing release mechanism at both acidic pH and physiological conditions. The obtained outcomes from release data revealed a controlled release behavior with a 22-hour half-life, while the EE% and EL% were acquired at 46.75 % and 87.5 %, respectively. In addition, the MTT assay has been carried out on U-87 MG cell lines to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the nanocomposite. The findings showed that the fabricated nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs can be assumed as a biocompatible CUR nanocarrier, while the drug-loaded nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs@CUR showed enhanced cytotoxicity compared to the pure CUR. Based on the obtained results, this study suggests the CS/GE/CQDs nanocomposite as a biocompatible and potential nanocarrier for ameliorating CUR delivery restrictions to treat brain cancers.

ce:section.ce:para: As technologies have advanced, risk factors such as smoking, air pollution, physical inactivity, and fast food that cause cancers have become widespread [1–3]. Thus, diverse types of cancers have emerged and made numerous people suffer. Scientists put a lot of effort into treating cancers, while conventional methods including surgery, radiotherapy, and traditional chemotherapy were ineffective or had serious side effects [4–6]. Therefore, researchers and scientists have developed novel drug delivery systems by exploiting nano and biotechnology sciences [7–9]. These novel drug delivery technologies can be applied to the chemotherapy process while the active agent is usually covered with a coating that can be fabricated purposefully to be sensitive to physical and chemical conditions [10–14]. This particular characteristic has enabled scientists to encapsulate anticancer drugs and control drug release in a stimuli-sensitive behavior [15–17].

In most cases, anticancer drugs are considerably toxic, which can cause a lot of side effects, and are poorly stable either physically or chemically, making the drugs ineffective on cancerous cells [18–20]. Novel drug delivery technology has encompassed these issues by providing higher drug retention with controlled drug release behavior as well as delivering the drug to the targeted tumor cells in a stimuli-sensitive approach [21–23]. However, some new restrictions have emerged including biocompatibility, solubility, and permeability that need to be ameliorated and optimized for treating cancers more efficiently [24–26]. In this regard, natural and hydrophilic biomaterials such as CS and GE can be potential substances for employment in drug delivery systems as a consequence of their remarkable biocompatibility and good solubility in an aqueous medium, which can reduce side effects and provide higher bioavailability during the chemotherapy process [27–30].

CS is a natural polymer, which belongs to the polysaccharide groups and is a derivative of chitin [31]. CS has a characteristic chemical structure, making this biopolymer soluble in a comprehensive range of pH conditions, from alkaline to acidic [32]. Furthermore, reactive hydroxyl and amino groups have turned CS into a potential biopolymer with remarkable film-forming capability [33,34]. It is also noteworthy that the altered density of the CS can be adjusted by means of changing the degree of acetylation and pH conditions [35–37]. This specific characteristic has introduced CS as a pH-sensitive biopolymer with great potential in designing novel drug delivery systems for delivering the drug to the targeted cells. In recent research, Aghazadeh Asl et al. [38], developed a biocompatible and biodegradable nanocarrier of Al-MOF/GO@5-FU/DOX which was coated by CS. They encapsulated DOX and 5-FU in the fabricated carrier with water in the oil emulsion technique. Their outcomes revealed that their synthesized nanocomposite had 71.2 % biodegradability after 30 days and they assigned this result to the CS incorporation. Furthermore, they achieved a higher drug release at an acidic pH of 5 in their study, which was related to the CS pH-sensitivity as the shell of their system.

GE is a water-soluble biomacromolecule that can be acquired from animal resources. There are two types of acid and alkaline-treated GE-based extraction technique [39]. The specific physio-chemical properties of GE such as viscosity, gel strength, temperature, and pH-dependent structure have made this protein a capable biomaterial for employment in diverse biomedical applications, especially drug delivery sciences [40–42]. In recent research, Hasanin et al. [43] designed a graphene-based carrier for pH-sensitive DOX delivery. Their drug-loaded GO/GE/GA@DOX nanocomposite has indicated 77 % drug release at acidic pH compared with 25 % release at normal pH conditions. Moreover, in vitro cytotoxicity assessments have been carried out on A549 and WI-38 as lung cancer and normal cell lines (fibroblast), respectively. The results showed that the fabricated GO/GE/GA nanocomposite had acceptable biocompatibility, which can be attributed to its biocompatible constituents and green synthesis process.

CS/GE combination can make a biocompatible, biodegradable, and water-soluble hydrogel in which solubility is significantly dependent on the pH condition [28]. The isoelectric pH for acidic GE is near 5 while CS is a polycation. When the pH range of 5.4–7.4 increases, it is anticipated that interactions between CS/GE will be fortified and drug release can be controlled. Zhou et al. [44] loaded 5-FU into GE and CS-based microspheres. These microspheres were prepared by a water-in-oil emulsion cross-linking method with a size of about 300 μm. Kim et al. [45] fabricated a CS/GE-based hydrogel in a chemo-photothermal therapeutic process to treat rheumatoid arthritis. Their outcomes revealed remarkable biocompatibility as well as a good gel-forming process, which led to a stable gel in the body. This study demonstrated that CS/GE hydrogel can be a potential nanocomposite for employment in diverse biomedical applications.

CQDs can be assumed as a rather new class of carbon nanomaterials. They have good conductivity, chemical, and thermal stability. Besides, CQDs have a 2D-3D structure with graphitic sp2 and sp3 hybridizations while their sizes are mostly <10 nm. These characteristic properties have made CQDs an applicable nanomaterial for employment in various biomedical applications, especially for designing novel drug delivery systems [46–48]. Although CS/GE hydrogel can be assumed as an applicable pH-sensitive carrier for delivering drugs, a considerable issue with its mechanical strength has restricted its biomedical application and lowered its efficiency [49,50]. CQDs have been introduced to the prepared CS-GE hydrogel to overcome this limitation and enhance overall efficacy for drug delivery applications. In recent work, Mansur et al. [51] employed CQDs for functionalizing carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) to deliver the DOX for treating brain cancer. They enhanced the mechanical and chemical stability of CMC by adding CQDs. Furthermore, their outcomes indicated that after in vivo analysis, 41 % tumor inhibitions were obtained, which shows that their systems were applicable for drug delivery applications.

CUR is a natural base anticancer drug, one of the most effective drugs against cancers such as prostate, colorectal, breast, and brain [52–54]. However, some noticeable limitations have restricted its application in traditional chemotherapy procedures. CUR has a low chemical stability in the body with a maximum half-life of 3 h [55]. Therefore, due to the weak solubility of CUR in water and as a result its low bioavailability because of its low absorption in tissues and rapid metabolism, the therapeutic use of CUR is limited. To solve this problem, CUR encapsulation was extensively discussed to develop its applications in the food and pharmaceutical industries [53,56]. CS with superior features, such as biodegradability and biocompatibility, is used in various forms such as self-assembled NPs, nanoemulsions, nanotubes, nanocomposites and nanofibers for CUR delivery [57,58]. To address these restrictions, the current work suggests a new pH-sensitive drug delivery system consisting of CS/GE/CQDs for encapsulating CUR, which is prepared with a double emulsion technique (W/O/W). This nanocomposite has been fabricated by employing Span-80 as an emulsifier and hazelnut oil as a hydrophobic phase. Several examinations have been conducted to consider the potential of constructed nanocomposites for employment in brain cancer treatment. At first, some characteristic analysis was performed, including FTIR and XRD, to investigate whether the drug has been encapsulated successfully in the prepared nanocarrier and look into the crystalline or amorphous structures of the nanocomposites. Afterward, DLS, Zeta potential, and FE-SEM morphology assessments were conducted to evaluate size distribution, interfacial charge, and images of the CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanocomposite. Then, in vitro drug release patterns in two pH conditions (pH 5.4 and 7.4) have been analyzed to investigate whether the fabricated system could make a controlled release pattern with a higher half-life for the loaded CUR. Moreover, the drug release kinetic model has been obtained by statistical analysis and employing curve fitting assessment on some renowned kinetic models in drug delivery science. At last, an in vitro cytotoxicity study has been implemented to indicate the efficacy of the synthesized drug-loaded nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs@CUR in the cancer therapy process. In this regard, an MTT assay has been carried out on U-87 MG cell lines to investigate the cytotoxicity effects on brain tumors.

GE type B with a water solubility of 50 mg·mL−1 has been purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). This product was obtained from bovine skin with an above 95 % protein content and a Bloom number of 225, which indicates a good gel-forming capability. Moreover, non-animal-derived CS with a degree of deacetylation of 75 % and an average Mw 50 kDa was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Furthermore, MTT powder, PBS, FCS, FBS, acetic acid (99.5 %), and Span-80 were obtained from Merck (Germany). Besides, CUR was obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). The deionized water has been employed to provide the entire solution purified by the Millipore-Q System (18.2 MΩ cm). It is worth mentioning that all the materials had an analytical grade and were employed unless further purification.

CQDs were fabricated through a hydrothermal technique based on the formerly published article [59,60]. First, diammonium hydrogen citrate (2 g) was dissolved in 75 mL of distilled water. Afterward, the prepared mixture was transported in an autoclave with a 150 mL volume, heated to reach 120 °C, and retained in that condition for 10 h. When the reaction was completed, the autoclave was exposed to the ambient temperature to be cooled naturally and gently. At last, centrifugation (10,000 rpm) was employed for 10 min and CQDs were acquired. Furthermore, the concentration of 27.1 mg. mL−1 for the fabricated CQDs from the citrate source was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CS/GE/CQDs and CUR loading process
ce:section.ce:para: As the first step, as reported in our previous report [46], 0.4 g CS in addition to 20 mL acetic acid (2 % v/v) was put on a heater stirrer to reach a homogenous CS solution (2 % w/v) at room temperature [61]. Afterward, 0.2 g GE was added to the prepared CS solution, the mixture was laid for 10 min in an ultrasonic bath. At that time, 20 mg of the previously synthesized CQDs was added to the solution agitating on the stirrer. At the next step, CUR loaded to acquire the final concentration of 5 μg·mL−1 for the hydrogel [62] and the prepared mixture agitated for an extra 30 min on the heater stirrer where finally the homogeneous hydrogel CS/GE/CQDs containing CUR was obtained. Afterward, 10 mL of the produced mixture was gently added to the 30 mL hazelnut oil as the hydrophobic phase and Span-80 (2 % v/v) added while the mixture intensely agitating on the heater stirrer. At a later 10 min, 30 mL of distilled water in place of the hydrophile phase was added drop by drop [63]. Then, the prepared emulsion took off from the stirrer and stayed for 15 min until the phase layers separated. Afterward, the middle layer which is mostly composed of CS/GE/CQDs@CUR, collected by a sampler and the centrifugation (4500 rpm) was carried out to segregate the drug-loaded nanocarrier from the aqueous phase for 10 min. In the last stage, the freeze dryer was employed to powder the samples. Samples were laid at −20 °C to become completely frozen, then lyophilized. Fig. 1 represents a summary of the synthetic operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of CUR encapsulation and loading efficiency (LE)
ce:section.ce:para:
To obtain drug LE and encapsulation efficiency (EE) in the synthesized nanocomposite (equations below), 1 mg of the lyophilized nanocomposite was collected and weighed precisely, then dispersed in 1 mL PBS. In the next step, 1 mL of ethyl acetate was added to this solution during it was stirring. The aim was to determine the unloaded drugs by separating the ethyl acetate phase, which represents the free CUR. The CUR concentration was determined with a UV–visible spectrophotometer at the 425 nm wavelength [64,65].

A dialysis bag with Mw 12 kD has been employed to investigate CUR release behavior in pH 7.4 and 5.4 from the CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanocomposite [66]. The pH has been set to 5.4 by employing acetic acid and PBS as the buffer solution to mimic the tumor microenvironment. Additionally, pH 7.4, as the alkaline pH condition, was prepared to represent the physiological pH condition. At first, the drug-loaded nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs@CUR was placed into a beaker, which was poured with water at 37 °C. Then, 1 mL from the prepared mixture was relocated into a dialysis bag and transferred into 15 mL falcons including pH 5.4 and 7.4 solutions. Afterward, 300 μL of the samples were removed at preset times of 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 6, 12, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h, and the same volumes of the same fresh solution were substituted at each timestep. Moreover, the control sample was the deionized water that was determined by a UV–visible spectrophotometer at wavelength 425 nm. Furthermore, the cumulative drug release acquired using CUR standard curve. In this regard, a statistical analysis was applied to in vitro drug release data to assess the drug release mechanism at various pH conditions. CUR release was obtained by employing the below equation [67]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic analysis in various pH conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental CUR release data from the synthesized nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs@CUR has been obtained at acidic and physiological pH conditions. The curve fitting method has been utilized to statistically analyze the drug release mechanism by fitting CUR release data on well-known kinetic models in drug delivery science [60,68]. Subsequently, the best-fitted kinetic models were analyzed on the basis of the highest acquired R2 values. Then, the governing CUR release mechanism from CS/GE/CQDs@CUR has been assessed. In this regard, Table 1 represents the kinetic models employed for assessing the CUR release mechanism in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture preparation and in vitro cytotoxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: A Malignant human glioma cell line (U-87 MG) was obtained from PI, Iran. To proliferate the cells at humidified conditions with 37 °C and 5 % CO2, 10 % FBS, and 1 % antibiotics have been employed [69]. In vitro cytotoxicity analysis has been performed for pure CUR, CS, CS/GE, CS/GE/CQDs, and CS/GE/CQDs@CUR on U-87 MG cell lines using MTT assay in 72 h.

In respect of the U-87 MG incubation process, it has taken one day to create >75 % confluency in cell-cell attachment. This process has been performed at the 5 ×
10 3
cells' density per wall in a 96-wall plate by employing 10 % FBS and 1 % antibiotic mixture medium (PEN and STR). The cultured cells incubated for 72 h with prepared samples. The control cells have received no treatment. Then, 100 μL of MTT reagent was transferred to the walls and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. Afterward, these were washed with PBS solution, then, 200 μL dimethyl sulfoxide was employed to improve the formazan crystals solvation. As a last stage, the prepared samples were shaken gently for about 15 min, cell viability was detected using an ELISA microplate reader at 570 nm wavelength. The following equation was employed to determine the cell viability percentage [70].

FTIR assessment as an elemental analysis can be used for detecting the existing functional groups, and atomic and intermolecular stretching vibrations [71,72]. In this study, the FTIR analysis of the drug-loaded nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs@CUR and its compositions was applied to investigate CUR encapsulation as well as constituents' incorporation into the fabricated drug-loaded nanocarrier. Fig. 2 represents the FTIR patterns of the provided materials. At first, based on the FTIR pattern of CS, which is the major component in the synthesized nanocarrier, four characteristic peaks were identified. The peak about 3351 cm−1 at the wavelength between 3000 cm−1 and 3600 cm−1 was assigned to the two functional groups of NH2 and OH stretching vibrations, while the peak about 2860 cm−1 can be related to CH stretching vibration. Moreover, the peak at 1587 cm−1 represents CO stretching vibration and the peak at 1022 cm−1 in the wavelength between 930 and 1100 cm−1 might show COC stretching vibration [72,73]. In the FTIR pattern of GE, there is a characteristic peak about 3469 cm−1 in the wavelength range of 3300–3700 cm−1. This peak can be related to the OH stretching vibration while the peak about 2923 cm−1 was indicated to CH and CH2 stretching vibrations. Furthermore, the peak about 1641 cm−1 can be attributed to CO stretching vibrations while the peaks about 1384 cm−1 and 1022 cm−1 might be attributed to amid II and III stretching vibrations, respectively [73,74]. Based on the FTIR pattern of CQDs, the peak at 3464 cm−1 at the wavelength between 3100 cm−1 and 3700 cm−1 denotes OH stretching vibration while the peak at 2927 cm−1 can be assigned to CH stretching vibration. Besides, the peak at a wavelength of 1631 cm−1 shows the CO stretching vibration and the peak at a wavelength of 1384 cm−1 might be attributed to CC stretching vibration. In the FTIR pattern of the synthesized CS/GE and the wavelength area bounded by 3000 cm−1 and 3800 cm−1, there is a peak at 3464 cm−1, which indicates both NH2 and OH stretching vibrations. This wavelength area has been intensified in comparison with the same wavelength area in the CS pattern, which can be attributed to the favorable miscibility between CS and GE. Moreover, the peak about 3000 cm−1 can be related to the CH and CH2 stretching vibrations that have become broader and sharper compared with the same wavelength area in both patterns of CS and GE. Besides, the peak at 1635 cm−1 which denotes CO stretching vibration has been intensified and moved to the higher wavelength area in comparison with the same wavelength area in CS and GE. The wavelength range 1000–1200 cm−1 and the peak at 1122 cm−1 that might be related to COC stretching vibration has been shifted and intensified compared with CS and GE patterns. After the addition of CQDs to the fabricated CS/GE hydrogel, the fingerprint section has not changed dramatically, the peak at 1635 cm−1 that indicated the CO remained the same. However, the wavelength area from 2570 cm−1 to 3580 cm−1 and the peak around 3077 cm−1, which can be related to amine and hydroxyl functional group vibrations, have been intensified and significantly broadened compared to the same wavelength in CS/GE. This result can be interpreted as the CQDs incorporation into the CS/GE hydrogel. The FTIR pattern of CUR as the active agent has been provided in Fig. 2. In this pattern, the narrow peak at 3510 cm−1 demonstrates OH stretching vibration while the peaks around 1511 cm−1 and 1592 cm−1 were assigned to CO and CC stretching vibrations, respectively. Moreover, the wavelength area from 1200 cm−1 to 1350 cm−1 and the peak around 1278 cm−1 might was related to COC stretching vibration [75,76]. After the addition of CUR to the fabricated nanocarrier of CS/GE/CQDs, the peak around 1631 cm−1, which is related to the CO stretching vibration, has shifted to the lower wavelength, the peak around 1118 cm−1 that might be related to the COC stretching vibration has been shifted and intensified dramatically. Furthermore, the most distinctive alteration has occurred in the wavelength area between 2360 cm−1 and 3598 cm−1, which was assigned to the OH, CH, CH2, and CH stretch in aromatics due to the incorporation of CUR in the prepared CS/GE/CQDs.

To investigate the crystalline properties of CS/GE/CQDs@CUR and its constituents, XRD analysis has been employed. According to Fig. 3 , the XRD pattern for CS represents a characteristic peak at 20.6°, which is quite intense. This peak has been located from 10° to 30°, indicating a rather broad range. Thereby, the consumed CS in this study has a semi-crystalline structure. It is also worth mentioning that the crystalline property of CS is directly associated with its degree of deacetylation [77,78]; in this case, the result approves 75 % deacetylation degree. Furthermore, based on the XRD pattern of GE, there are two broad peaks at 2θ = 21° and 39.5°, which show an amorphous structure for GE. This result compromises with other GE XRD assessments [79]. Moreover, there is a broad characteristic peak at 2θ = 26° with low intensity in the CQDs XRD model that can be assigned to the material's amorphous structure. After adding GE to CS and performing XRD analysis, the obtained pattern of CS/GE indicates a broad characteristic peak at 2θ = 21° same as CS and GE but with much lower intensity. This result can be assigned to the good miscibility between CS and GE with an amorphous structure. Afterward, by adding CQDs to CS/GE hydrogel, an intense peak at 2θ = 27.6° emerged, representing the CQDs incorporation into the hydrogel. Furthermore, the characteristic peak at 2θ = 22° has intensified and attained 1370 compared with the same peak in CS/GE. This outcome shows that the amorphous structure of CS/GE/CQDs has been enhanced after the addition of CQDs. At last, after the addition of CUR to the prepared CS/GE/CQDs, the characteristic peak at 2θ = 22° was broadened and significantly intensified (2170 a.u.) compared to the CS/GE/CQDs pattern. This outcome demonstrates an improvement in the amorphous structure after the addition of CUR to the prepared nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs.

The interfacial charges have been investigated by Zeta potential analysis, which can lead to electrostatic repulsions and bring more and longer stability for the fabricated CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanocomposite. The outcome of this analysis has been provided in Fig. 4.a, which demonstrates a positive interfacial charge between +25 mV and +75 mV with a mean value of +55 mV. These results are all above +20 mV, which is a critical value [80,81], therefore, the fabricated drug-loaded nanocomposites can be considered as stable and well-dispersed particles. Furthermore, DLS analysis has been done to define the size distribution of the fabricated nanocomposite. The result of this test is represented in Fig. 4.b, which indicates a quite narrow distribution range with a sharp peak at 218.6 nm that can be assumed as the mean diameter. This obtained value for diameter distribution and mean diameter can be considered in the acceptable range for drug delivery purposes [82]. Moreover, FE-SEM analysis has been utilized to investigate the visual characteristics of the fabricated CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanocomposites. The FE-SEM images have been represented in Fig. 5 , which indicate a virtually spherical shape with smooth and well-grounded interfacial layers. Besides, a homogeneous distribution can be observed for the drug-loaded nanocomposites, which was expected from the zeta potential results.

CUR can be applied as one of the most appropriate drugs for treating diverse kinds of cancers. However, CUR suffers from low chemical stability in the body, which leads to a low half-life in the circulatory system [83,84]. To overcome this major challenge, the encapsulation and loading capacities can be vitally important to improve the general efficacy of the drug delivery process and cancer therapy. The current study employed CS/GE/CQDs as the nanocarrier for encapsulating CUR. This system's drug loading and encapsulation efficiency acquired 46.75 % and 87.5 %, respectively, which are acceptable values for drug delivery applications [85,86]. In this regard, Table 2 indicates the fabricated pH-sensitive systems for CUR delivery.

An in vitro drug release investigation was done by means of a dialysis approach at acidic and physiological conditions to examine the pH-sensitive CUR release from the nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs@CUR. It is also worth mentioning that these experiments have been carried out at the normal body temperature of 37 °C, while pH 7.4 and 5.4 were selected to simulate the normal pH of the blood and tumor conditions, respectively [87]. According to Fig. 6 and at pH 5.4, there is a burst release (35 %) in the first 3 h of the whole release period, while this amount at pH 7.4 was only 16 % with a gradual pattern. Moreover, after 12 h, more than half of CUR's content (56 %) has been released at pH 5.4, while the half-life of the CUR has been obtained at 22 h at pH 7.4. Considering depicted in Fig. 6, the drug release patterns at each pH have become steadier with an increasing gradual trend and almost equal slopes after their half-lives. Finally, CUR was released above 98 % after 96 h of the experiment period, while this amount was 81 % at pH 7.4 in the same timescale. Based on the overall trends of the provided patterns in Fig. 6, it can be vividly found that CUR release at acidic conditions was substantially higher than phycological pH, which implies the pH-sensitive characteristics of the created CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanocomposite. These results reveal a complete CUR release at tumor pH, stating an increased circulation time and improved controlled release pattern. Besides, unwanted effects were remarkably decreased by reducing the destruction of healthy cells rather than cancer cells. In recent research, Wu and coworkers [88], fabricated carrier-free CUR nanoparticles to investigate their antioxidant potential and biomedical capabilities. Their fabricated nanoparticles reached 50 % release after 6 h, while this amount was 44 % in our case, which represents a more gradual release behavior. In another research, Saima and coworkers [89], developed a CUR-based self-emulsifying drug delivery nanocomposite in wound healing applications. Their obtained release patterns indicated about 90 % release after 48 h, while the acquired CUR release in our study was 81 % and 65 % at acidic and normal pH conditions, respectively.

The kinetic assessment has been carried out using statistical analysis on the obtained release data from the dialysis method at physiological conditions (pH 7.4) and tumor environment (pH 5.4). For the purpose of determining the predominant CUR release mechanism from the fabricated CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanocomposite, release data were fitted on zero and first-order, Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas, and Hixon-Crowell kinetic models. As mentioned earlier, the curve fitting model was utilized to analyze the release behavior of CUR, while merely the meaningful parameters such as R-squared values and kinetic characteristics were investigated and the results were provided in Fig. 7 and Table 3 . According to Fig. 7 and the obtained outcomes in Table 3, the best-fitted kinetic models were determined first-order at pH 5.4 and Higuchi with the highest R2 values equal to 0.98 at 7.4, respectively. These results show that at physiological pH conditions, dissolution and diffusion are the governing release mechanisms and diffusion is the rate-limiting phase in this process, while the release behavior at tumor pH conditions is directly dependent on concentration and the concentration proportioned release can be assumed as the predominant mechanism. It is also worth considering the Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic results in which, at acidic pH and pH 7.4, the characteristic exponent has been acquired 0.56 and 0.83, respectively. These amounts are in the range of 0.43–0.85, which is attributed to anomalous transport. Thereby, anomalous transport, which is a non-Fickian diffusion mechanism, can be assumed as the governing release mechanism in both pH conditions.

MTT assay as a useful cytotoxicity assessment has been carried out on U-87 MG cell lines for a 72-h incubation period to investigate whether the synthesized drug-loaded nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs@CUR can be effectually viewed as acting to inhibit the proliferation of the brains tumors and destroying them. According to Fig. 8 , the cell viability results for the samples of pure CUR, CS, CS/GE, CS/GE/CQDs, and CS/GE/CQDs@CUR are provided. It is also worth mentioning that the utilized concentration for each sample was 5 μg·mL−1. The obtained outcomes reveal that CS and physically crosslinked hydrogel of CS/GE are biocompatible. After the addition of CQDs to the prepared hydrogel the cytotoxicity has not changed considerably. Therefore, the fabricated nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs can be assumed as a biocompatible nanocarrier with above 95 % cell viability. Furthermore, after the CUR incorporation into the CS/GE/CQDs nanocarrier, the inhibitory effects on U-87 MG cancer cells reached 50 % cell viability, which is 10 % more cytotoxic than pure CUR. Thus, the fabricated nanocarrier can be considered a potential CUR delivery system to treat brain cancers favorably.

The current research was dedicated to introducing CQDs into the physically crosslinked CS/GE hydrogel by employing water-in-oil-in-water (W/O/W) double emulsion technique. The fabricated nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs was employed as a nanocarrier for CUR delivery with a controlled release template in a pH-sensitive model. The statistical analysis of CUR release data from the CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanocomposite revealed over 96 h of drug retention with the highest obtained half-life of 22 h. Furthermore, after CUR encapsulation into the prepared nanocarrier, some characteristic assessments, such as FTIR and XRD, have been carried out to evaluate the CUR incorporation into the system. Moreover, based on the acquired XRD results, an improvement has occurred in the amorphous structure of the synthesized CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanocomposite after the addition of CUR to the prepared nanocarrier. Afterward, the morphology analysis was performed by employing DLS and Zeta potential analysis, which indicated an appropriate size distribution range with 218.6 nm mean diameter and +55 mV as a mean value for the interfacial charges, which demonstrated good stability between CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanoparticles. Besides, the provided FE-SEM images indicated a homogeneous distribution of nanoparticles with a quite smooth and spherical structure, which can be attributed to good compatibility among constituents in the drug-loaded nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs@CUR. Then, the in vitro drug release assessment and kinetic analysis were employed at pH 5.4 and 7.4. The CUR-loaded delivery system has shown a pH-sensitive behavior while the LE and EE have been obtained at 46.75 % and 87.5 %, respectively. Moreover, the kinetic analysis, which was carried out using statistical analysis on release data determined the first-order and Higuchi delivery mechanisms as the most-fitted kinetic models at acidic and physiological conditions, respectively. The MTT assay was carried out on U-87 MG cell lines to examine the cytotoxicity effects of the fabricated CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanocomposite on treating brain cancers. The obtained outcomes revealed an enhanced apoptotic effect of CS/GE/CQDs@CUR nanocomposite in comparison with the pure CUR. The obtained results introduce the fabricated pH-sensitive nanocomposite of CS/GE/CQDs as a biocompatible and efficient nanocarrier for improving the restrictions of CUR delivery systems.
Shima Ostovar: Data curation, Methodology, Writing - original draft
Mehrab Pourmadadi: Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision, review & editing
Amin Shamsabadipour: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing -review & editing
Parsa Mashayekh: Data curation, Methodology, Writing - original draft
